summary.category: Pre-Modern Economies: Agriculture, Crafts, and Trade
points: Pre-modern livelihoods were primarily agrarian; peasants' relationships to land varied (landowners, tenants, sharecroppers, labourers) and were shaped by feudal systems (Europe), caste hierarchies (Asia), and property laws.
Feudal manorial system bound serfs to land, requiring labour services to lords in exchange for subsistence; peasants supplemented agriculture with livestock, forest products, fishing, and crafts.

Craft production through guilds (Europe) and castes (Asia) provided crucial livelihoods; Indian textiles, metalwork, and ceramics were globally renowned; family workshops transmitted skills across generations.

Long-distance trade networks (Silk Roads, Indian Ocean, Mediterranean) connected regions; merchant communities maintained diaspora networks; trade was often dominated by specific ethnic or religious groups.
European colonialism restructured economies to extract resources and subordinate colonies to metropolitan interests through coerced labour, land dispossession, and enforcement of dependency.

British colonial policy de-industrialised India: destroyed textile production (employing millions of weavers), imposed tariffs on Indian goods, flooded markets with cheap British manufactures, and promoted cash crop production.

Colonial plantation systems (sugar Caribbean, tea India, rubber Southeast Asia) worked by enslaved or indentured labourers; workers earned minimal wages, faced brutal conditions, and were perpetually indebted through plantation stores.

Colonial cities created new wage labour forms (domestic service, construction, commerce) concentrating rural migrants in harsh urban conditions; women concentrated in lowest-paid occupations with intense gender-based exploitation.

summary.category: The Atlantic Slave Trade and Slavery (16th–19th centuries)
points: The Atlantic slave trade forcibly transported approximately 12 million Africans to Americas; enslavement was brutal—people were violently torn from communities, shipped in horrific conditions, sold as property, with no rights or compensation.

Slavery generated enormous wealth for planters, merchants, and manufacturers while producing nothing but suffering for the enslaved; wealth from slavery financed industrialisation in Europe through brutal plantation labour.

Enslaved people resisted continuously through work slowdowns, escapes, sabotage, and rebellions (Haitian Revolution 1791–1804 demonstrated enslaved peoples' revolutionary potential); yet slavery persisted until nineteenth-century abolition.

Abolition (Britain 1833, USA 1865) did not end racial exploitation; former enslaved people faced severe discrimination and economic subordination; racial inequalities established through slavery persisted through segregation and denied redistributive justice.

summary.category: The Industrial Revolution and Transformation of Labour
points: Industrialisation (beginning late 18th-century Britain) mechanised production, creating unemployment among craftspeople and peasants; enclosure movements and land consolidation forced peasants into cities as wage labourers.

Factory system subjected workers to machine discipline, repetitive deskilled tasks, long hours (12–16 daily), low wages, dangerous machinery without safety, child labour, and no compensation for injuries.

Factory concentrations enabled worker organisation; early workers formed combinations (unions), held strikes, engaged in machine-breaking; governments suppressed unions, strikes, and collective action through violence and imprisonment.

Industrialised agriculture mechanised production and consolidated landholdings, reducing labour needs and creating agricultural labourers; cheap grain from North America and Russia impoverished European peasants in late nineteenth century.

summary.category: Gender and Women's Livelihoods Across Historical Systems
points: In pre-modern economies, women performed essential agricultural and craft labour but had limited property rights and economic autonomy; women's work was often unrecognised or unpaid, mediated through male relatives.

Industrialisation created factory employment for women at lower wages than men; factories concentrated women in textiles where machinery was hazardous; women faced double burden of factory work plus unpaid household/childcare labour.

Ideology of separate spheres (women in domestic sphere, men in public) was reinforced during industrialisation; women's wages treated as supplementary; married women excluded from many occupations despite working.

Colonialism had specific gender impacts: undermined women's productive resource access, commercialised agriculture away from women's control, destroyed traditional craft income sources, concentrated women in lowest-paid colonial occupations (domestic service, garment work).

summary.category: Contemporary Issues: Globalisation and Environmental Limits
points: Late twentieth-century globalisation outsourced manufacturing to low-wage countries, deindustrialised wealthy nations, created complex global supply chains, and expanded precarious informal employment without protections or security.

Women concentrated in global garment supply chains as poorly paid seamstresses in dangerous conditions; global agricultural trade displaced peasant farmers as cheap imported food flooded local markets forcing rural-to-urban migration.

Contemporary economies founded on unlimited growth assumptions confront environmental limits: climate change threatens agricultural livelihoods, deforestation and pollution undermine resource-dependent communities, poverty persists despite growth.

Current economic inequalities build on centuries of slavery, colonialism, and labour coercion; alternative economic approaches emphasising sustainability, equity, and meeting human needs are being developed by communities and movements worldwide.
